did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses deep learning and convolutional neural network (CNN) models to classify skin cancer images. Specifically, it employs a custom CNN, ResNet 50, AlexNet, GoogleNet V3 Inception, and Vgg16 models to perform binary classification (benign vs. malignant) on skin cancer datasets from the ISIC repository.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental results, including the implementation and evaluation of multiple deep learning models (CNN, ResNet 50, AlexNet, GoogleNet V3 Inception, Vgg16) on the ISIC skin cancer dataset. It reports specific performance metrics such as accuracy, loss, and epoch-wise validation results, which characterizes it as an original technical study rather than a review.

title: Epoch interrogation for skin cancer detection using convolutional neural network models
journal: IOP Conference Series: Materials Science and Engineering
brief_summary: This study evaluates deep learning models for binary classification of skin cancer (benign vs. malignant) using datasets from the ISIC repository. The authors implemented and compared five convolutional neural network architectures: a custom CNN, ResNet 50, AlexNet, GoogleNet V3 Inception, and Vgg16. Performance was assessed by training models on varying numbers of images and monitoring accuracy, loss, and validation metrics over specific epoch counts. The Vgg16 model achieved the highest classification accuracy of 97.3% with 5636 samples, followed by GoogleNet V3 Inception at 96%. The results indicate that increasing the number of training samples generally improves accuracy, though care must be taken to avoid overfitting.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study uses a dataset from the ISIC repository containing 1800 images of benign moles for binary classification against malignant lesions.
The study uses a dataset from the ISIC repository containing 1497 images of malignant classified moles for binary classification against benign lesions.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin cancer (benign vs. malignant)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs binary classification to predict whether a skin lesion is benign or malignant using datasets from the ISIC repository. Models evaluated include a custom CNN, ResNet 50, AlexNet, GoogleNet V3 Inception, and Vgg16. Performance was measured using classification accuracy, with the Vgg16 model achieving 97.3% accuracy on 5636 samples.

datasets_used.name: ISIC Archive
datasets_used.description: A dataset from the ISIC (International Skin Imaging Collaboration) repository containing dermoscopic images of skin lesions. The study utilized a subset of this dataset comprising individual patient records.

use.training: Used as the primary source for training various CNN models (Custom CNN, ResNet 50, AlexNet, GoogleNet V3 Inception, Vgg16) with varying sample sizes (e.g., 1300, 3297, 3374, 5636 samples) across different experimental setups.
use.validation: Used for validation during model training (e.g., 26 samples for one ResNet 50 setup, 149 samples for Vgg16 and AlexNet setups).
use.testing: Used for final classification accuracy evaluation (e.g., 5636 samples for Vgg16, 3374 for GoogleNet, 3297 for Custom CNN).

ISIC Skin Cancer Dataset (Custom CNN).notes: The model was trained on 3297 images. Table 1 provides epoch-wise metrics for 50 epochs.

ISIC Skin Cancer Dataset (ResNet 50).notes: The paper attributes the lower accuracy to the 'less number of images used for classification' (1000 samples).

ISIC Skin Cancer Dataset (AlexNet).notes: Feature extraction was done using AlexNet with the fully connected layer replaced by a convolutional layer.

ISIC Skin Cancer Dataset (GoogleNet V3 I.notes: Feature extraction was done using GoogLeNet Inception v3.

ISIC Skin Cancer Dataset (Vgg16).notes: The abstract rounds this to 97.3%. This was the highest accuracy achieved in the study.

architecture_used.main_model: Five models were evaluated: a custom CNN, ResNet 50, AlexNet, GoogleNet V3 Inception, and Vgg16.
architecture_used.pretraining: Not explicitly reported for the custom CNN. Transfer learning was implied for ResNet 50, AlexNet, GoogleNet V3 Inception, and Vgg16 as they are standard pre-trained architectures, but specific pretraining datasets (e.g., ImageNet) were not detailed in the text.
architecture_used.input_size: Not explicitly reported. Images were normalized by dividing RGB values by 255.

layers: Convolutional layers with kernel filter matrix
Flatten layer to remodel feature maps into 1D vector
custom_cnn.implementation: Built using Keras Sequential API, adding one layer at a time.

resnet_50.notes: Output shape of layers shown in Figure 9.

alexnet.modification: Fully connected layer replaced by a convolutional layer for feature extraction.

googlenet_v3_inception.notes: Feature extraction done using GoogLeNet Inception v3.

vgg16.notes: Output shape of layers shown in Figure 12.

training_details.loss_function: Not explicitly reported (Step Loss and Validation Loss metrics were tracked).

classification_or_output_head.details: The custom CNN used a flatten layer followed by classification. Specific classifier head architectures (e.g., Dense layer units) were not detailed for the custom CNN, nor were the final classification heads for the transfer learning models explicitly described beyond their standard definitions.

preprocessing_techniques: Normalization of RGB values by dividing all values by 255
conclusions: The Vgg16 model achieved the highest classification accuracy of 97.3% (specifically 97.32%) when trained on 5636 samples, outperforming other tested architectures.
GoogleNet V3 Inception achieved a classification accuracy of 96% with 3374 images.
The custom CNN model achieved a classification accuracy of 94.83% with 3297 images.
ResNet 50 achieved a classification accuracy of 90.78%, which the authors attribute to the limited number of images (1000) used for classification in that specific setup.
AlexNet achieved a classification accuracy of 81.8% with 1300 images, utilizing a modified architecture where the fully connected layer was replaced by a convolutional layer for feature extraction.

Increasing the number of training samples generally correlates with improved classification accuracy, as evidenced by the superior performance of Vgg16 with the largest sample size (5636) compared to ResNet 50 with the smallest (1000).
Deep learning models, particularly CNNs, are effective for binary classification of skin cancer (benign vs. malignant) using datasets from the ISIC repository.
important_remarks: The study is limited to a binary classification task (benign vs. malignant) using only two classes (1800 benign, 1497 malignant) from the ISIC repository, lacking the complexity of multi-class skin cancer diagnosis.

There is no evidence of external validation; the models were evaluated on subsets of the same ISIC dataset used for training, raising concerns about generalizability to independent clinical populations.
The paper does not report critical hyperparameters such as batch size, learning rate, or optimizer details, limiting reproducibility.
No data augmentation techniques were employed, which may contribute to the risk of overfitting, particularly for models with fewer training samples (e.g., ResNet 50 with 1000 samples).
The dataset size is relatively small compared to standard benchmarks for skin cancer detection, and the paper acknowledges that limited labeled images are a challenge in this domain.

The authors note that if the test dataset is known in advance, there is a risk of adapting the CNN classifier to the test dataset, suggesting potential data leakage or overfitting to the specific split used.

The paper highlights that many prior analysts did not specify train/test splits or used unequal images, leading to biased classification, but does not explicitly detail the stratification or randomization methods used in this study's splits.
The study is a conference paper published in IOP Conference Series, which may have less rigorous peer review compared to high-impact medical journals.

The authors warn that deep learning apps may cause harm to patients by misleading them with incorrect diagnoses, acknowledging the clinical stakes but not providing confidence intervals or statistical significance tests for the reported accuracies.
